summary: My name is Toyokichi Oka, born in Wakayama City on April 26, 1894. I grew up in a farming family and came to America in 1910, following my father who had arrived in 1900. I have fond memories of swimming and tennis in my youth, and I survived the Spanish flu pandemic while living in Vacaville, California. My experiences shaped my Christian faith deeply, especially during times of hardship.

I was born and raised in Wakayama City, specifically in a village that is now called Kitajima-Cho. It was a quiet place near the Kino River where I spent my childhood swimming and playing tennis. Wakayama was also where I attended school for eight years before coming to America. The local culture and my upbringing there influenced my early life deeply.

My father moved to Florin, California in 1900 where he farmed strawberries and grapes. Although I did not come with him initially, I joined him in America later in 1910. Florin was the place where my family began our life in the United States, and it represented a new chapter for us after leaving Japan.

My parents were followers of Tenrikyo, which was the religion practiced in my family back in Japan. My mother was very devout and prayed loudly every morning and evening to the God of Heaven for our wellbeing. Her faith was a significant influence on me, and it eventually led me to embrace Christianity later in life.

I remember a history teacher telling us that Jimmu Tennoh, the first Emperor of Japan, came from Judea and flew over Okinawa. This was a bold claim at the time and quite memorable for me. The teacher explained that Jimmu conquered native tribes and that the Japanese people have some Judean blood through him.

According to a history teacher I had, Amaterasu Ohmikami, a deity who came before Emperor Jimmu, originated from Judea. The teacher said that the natives in Japan saw Amaterasu as a winged figure from heaven because he flew over Okinawa. This story was quite striking and unusual for the time.

My history teacher claimed that the ancestors of the Japanese, including Amaterasu and Emperor Jimmu, came from Judea. He said that even Japanese clothing and language had roots tracing back to Judea. This idea was bold and left a strong impression on me during my schooling in Japan.

Okinawa was mentioned by my history teacher as the place over which Amaterasu and Emperor Jimmu flew when coming to Japan from Judea. The natives there interpreted these figures as winged beings from heaven. This detail was part of a larger story about the origins of the Japanese people that I learned in school.

My history teacher told us about the Kampei Taisha Shrine where the elder brother of Emperor Jimmu is enshrined. There are seven such shrines in Japan, and they are connected to the story of Jimmu's conquest of native tribes. This was part of the narrative linking Japanese imperial lineage to Judea.

The village where I grew up is now called Kitajima-Cho in Wakayama City. It was a quiet suburb near the Kino River and a bridge where I used to play and swim. This place holds many memories of my childhood before I came to America.

The Kino River was very close to my home in Wakayama, and I attended a swimming school there for eight years. In summer, the school was bustling with about a thousand students. I learned many swimming techniques and spent much of my youth playing in the water near the river.

During the Spanish flu pandemic, I was living in Vacaville, California. I was chosen by the Methodist Church there to help care for the sick in a temporary hospital. I caught the flu myself and was hospitalized, but I survived after a very difficult time, which deeply strengthened my Christian faith.

The Methodist Church in Vacaville played a crucial role during the Spanish flu pandemic. They selected me to help care for the sick in a temporary hospital. It was there, amidst suffering and death, that I found strength in my Christian faith and survived a severe bout of the flu.

I experienced the Spanish flu pandemic while living in Vacaville, California during World War I. Many people around me were falling ill, and I was chosen to help care for them. I caught the flu myself and was very sick with a 105°F fever, but I survived and it was a turning point in my life and faith.

During World War I, I was in Vacaville and witnessed the impact of the Spanish flu pandemic. The war ended on November 11, and I remember the bell tolling and the nurse telling me the fever was gone and the war was over. This time was very significant for me as I was recovering from the flu and reflecting on my life.

When I was very sick with the Spanish flu and hospitalized, I opened the Bible to find comfort and calm. Despite a high fever and the chaos around me, reading the Bible helped me face death without fear. This experience deepened my Christian faith profoundly.

I was about 10 years old when the Russo-Japanese War broke out. I remember walking around the village on my mother's back carrying a Japanese flag after Japan won the war. The whole village was buzzing with excitement over the victory against Russia.

I do not remember much about the Sino-Japanese War since I was very young at the time. I think many people were killed, but I was too young to have clear memories of it. It was not a significant part of my personal recollections.

When I was about 10 years old, my mother took me to Honzan, the Central Buddhist Temple, to listen to a three-day sermon by a priest from the Imperial family. The meeting was very emotional for many people, though I was too young to understand it fully. On the way back, I saw some attendees drinking and singing, which made me think about the nature of religion.

I attended a sermon at Honzan where a priest from the Imperial family preached for three days. The event was very moving for many attendees who expressed their gratitude with tears. This connection to the Imperial House of Japan was impressive to me as a child, even though I did not fully understand it at the time.
